feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

sections.title: Less Regulation, More Trust, More Market
path: Less Regulation, More Trust, More Market

paragraphs.text: dutch startup association

section_path: Less Regulation, More Trust, More Market
paragraphs.text: Subject: Consultation Response – Digital Fairness Act
The Dutch Startup Association (dSa), representing startups and scaleups across the Netherlands, welcomes fairness and trust in the digital market.
But Europe does not need more rules — it needs less rules, and better rules.

Startups are drowning in complexity. Every year brings new regulations, guidelines, and reporting templates layered on top of each other. What began as a promise of a Digital Single Market is turning into a compliance maze. Look at the current struggles of the 28th Regime. The Digital Fairness Act would just be another layer of red tape.
If the EU wants to promote fairness, it should start by enforcing the extensive consumer protection laws that already exist — not by creating new ones.
Simplification or Stagnation

Europe's startups operate under one of the most comprehensive consumer frameworks in the world — GDPR, Consumer Rights Directive, Unfair Commercial Practices Directive, CPC Regulation, DSA, and DMA already cover data use, advertising, contracts, and online fairness.
The DFA risks repeating rules that already exist. Instead of improving clarity, it could multiply confusion and administrative cost — especially for early-stage companies that lack compliance teams.
A startup-friendly approach means:
No duplication. The DFA should focus on coordination, simplification and enforcement, not new prohibitions.
One interpretation, not 27. Enforcement must be harmonised across Member States.
A “Startup Impact Check.” Every new digital rule should pass a test for necessity and proportionality before adoption.
If simplification isn't built in, the Digital Fairness Act will undermine the very competitiveness it seeks to protect.
Targeted Advertising Is not the problem — It's the solution
For startups, digital advertising is a life enabling tool.
It's how they reach their first customers, test ideas (A/B testing), and scale efficiently — often without marketing budgets.
Data shows that:
Targeted ads bring 162% more new and returning customers than contextual ads.
Restrictions increase customer acquisition costs by over 100%.
86% of EU Startups/SMEs report higher revenue thanks to personalised ads.
If the DFA limits legitimate, consent-based advertising, the only winners will be large incumbents (dinosaurs and/or zombies) with brand recognition and deep pockets.
Policy priorities:
Keep all GDPR legal bases, including legitimate interest.
Reject browser-level or OS-level consent controls that would turn Big Tech into new gatekeepers.
Protect personalised, privacy-respecting advertising as a core enabler of fair competition and consumer choice.
Regulation should empower startups to compete — not block the tools that make competition possible. Don't bulldoze over startups.
Free trials build trust, not harm
Free trials are one of the few tools startups can use to win consumer trust without spending heavily on advertising.
They allow users to try before they buy — a win for transparency and affordability.

But proposed DFA obligations (e.g. repeated confirmations, multiple reminders, complex cancellation flows) would cripple this model. Startups cannot come to life and survive if they must design processes for hypothetical risks instead of real consumer harm.
We urge the Commission to:
Recognise free trials as legitimate, transparent marketing, not a threat.
Avoid turning simple business models into legal minefields.
Protect consumers, yes — but don't destroy fair business models in the process. Startups are smart and know what to do.
Focus on enforcement, not expansion
Europe's problem is not a lack of rules — it's a lack of consistent enforcement and legal certainty.
Before inventing new categories like "digital fairness," the EU should fix enforcement gaps in existing frameworks.
Startups face:
Overlapping audits and reporting under the DSA, DMA, CRA, and NIS2.
Conflicting interpretations between national authorities.
Legal uncertainty that delays product launches and discourages investment.
The DFA should:
Streamline existing frameworks rather than expanding them.
Deliver clear, accessible guidance rather than new obligations.
Make simplification a measurable outcome, not a slogan.
Regulate behavior, not technology
The DFA must distinguish between manipulative practices that cause harm and normal user experience design that enables engagement.
Vague definitions of “dark patterns” or “addictive design” risk penalising harmless or even beneficial product features.
The Commission should:
Define manipulative design based on intent and proven harm, not aesthetics.
Avoid subjective judgments on what is “too engaging.”
Coordinate with the DSA and AI Act to ensure consistent, predictable rules.
Europe should not regulate creativity or usability out of its digital market.
One Goal: a fair, competitive market focused on winning
The EU should serve both consumers and innovators/startups. A fair market is one where small companies can compete, not just one where consumers are protected.
If compliance becomes unaffordable, fairness disappears — replaced by consolidation.
To ensure balance, the EU must:
Scale obligations by company size and risk.
Use enforcement and education, not bureaucracy, to build trust.
Involve startup associations and SME bodies in every stage of rulemaking.
What the EU should avoid
The fastest way to lose startup trust in this process is to:
Create new obligations disguised as clarifications.
Allow fragmented national rules to creep back in.
Shift consent power to big platforms.
Expand definitions without clear necessity or evidence.
If the DFA becomes a catch-all law for “everything digital,” it will fail to deliver fairness — for both consumers and innovators.

Startups are not asking for more rules — they are asking for fewer, clearer, and better-enforced ones. The existing EU framework already covers consumer protection, data use, advertising, contracts, and platform fairness. Adding another layer will not make the system fairer; it will make it slower, more fragmented, and less competitive.

The DFA, as currently conceived, risks becoming yet another instrument of regulatory inflation — well-intentioned on paper but damaging in practice. Every new obligation adds cost, uncertainty, and delay for the very innovators Europe depends on.
If the Commission proceeds regardless, it must focus exclusively on the priorities outlined above:
Simplify, don't duplicate. Enforce what exists instead of writing new rules.
Protect targeted advertising and free trials as legitimate tools for competition and consumer choice.
Apply strict proportionality — obligations must scale with company size and actual risk.
Harmonise enforcement to prevent a 27-speed Europe.
But make no mistake: the most "fair" outcome for startups, consumers, and Europe's competitiveness would be no new law at all.
Fairness cannot come from another layer of compliance — it must come from clarity, coherence, and trust in the rules we already have.
hendrik-jan@dutchstartupassociation.nl

Europe's digital policy should serve one clear goal: enabling the creation of Europe's first €1 trillion company. To achieve this, the EU must focus on completing the Single Market through a fully functioning 28th Regime — one company form, one digital registry, and once-only compliance across borders. The Dutch Startup Association stands firmly behind this vision: a Europe where ambitious founders can scale globally without leaving the continent.
Dutch Startup Association is the largest, independent representative for startups, scaleups and innovation in The Netherlands.
Member of:
Allied FOR STARTUPS
